ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ondeh Ondeh Cake; mapped to broader 'cake food product' (FOODON_03315091) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Ondeh Ondeh Cake; A0CKP (Cakes and pastries) used as the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Ondeh Ondeh Cake or a sufficiently close analogue; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard slice of Ondeh Ondeh Cake weighs approximately 150 g, representing a typical single-serve dessert portion as listed on GrabFood.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Pandan Cake; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Cake, sponge (FDC ID 18101) and Coconut milk, raw (FDC ID 12117)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Ondeh Ondeh Cake' as a composite dish. The primary reference is the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pandan Cake, which shares the core pandan sponge base. This is supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sponge cake (FDC ID 18101) and raw coconut milk (FDC ID 12117) to account for the gula melaka coconut filling and coconut topping. The input record values (originally from a GrabFood commercial nutritional panel) are internally consistent with this formulation and have been retained as the best available estimate after cross-checking against these two approved sources. Source match type is 'adapted'; confidence is 'medium'.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021: a systematic review. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Analogous estimate based on sponge cake with coconut milk and palm sugar.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ondeh Ondeh Cake in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated based on the closest analogous food: sponge cake with coconut milk and palm sugar filling, which falls in the medium GI range (GI approximately 60–70) per Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625–1632) and Foster-Powell et al. 2002 (Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 43.33 × 1.5) / 100 = 42.2 g GL per 150 g slice.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (30 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; saturated fat (13.3 g/100 g) from coconut milk further stiffens arterial walls
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid Ondeh Ondeh Cake: the 30 g/100 g sugar load triggers insulin-mediated sodium retention and the 13.3 g/100 g saturated fat from coconut milk independently elevates arterial stiffness, compounding cardiovascular risk beyond what sodium alone (166.7 mg/100 g) would suggest.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 30 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; medium GI of 65 combined with high carbohydrate density (43.3 g/100 g) and low fibre (2 g/100 g) produces a very high glycaemic load of approximately 42 per standard slice
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Ondeh Ondeh Cake entirely: a single 150 g slice delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 42, driven by 30 g/100 g sugar from gula melaka and added sugar, 43.3 g/100 g total carbohydrates, and only 2 g/100 g dietary fibre, causing rapid and sustained postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 13.3 g/100 g (predominantly from coconut milk) and trans fat at 0.33 g/100 g elevate LDL cholesterol; cholesterol content of 66.7 mg/100 g and high sugar load further increase atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should strictly limit or avoid Ondeh Ondeh Cake: the 13.3 g/100 g saturated fat from coconut milk raises LDL cholesterol, the 0.33 g/100 g trans fat adds independent atherogenic risk, and the 30 g/100 g sugar load promotes triglyceride synthesis, collectively creating a high cardiovascular risk profile per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 13.3 g/100 g from coconut milk exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; refined sugar at 30 g/100 g promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Ondeh Ondeh Cake: the 13.3 g/100 g saturated fat from coconut milk (primary fat source, not a coconut-oil carve-out) and 30 g/100 g refined sugar synergistically upregulate TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways, worsening joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ondeh Ondeh Cake is a Singaporean-Peranakan fusion dessert cake inspired by the traditional ondeh ondeh glutinous rice ball. It features layers of soft, pandan-flavoured sponge cake filled and coated with gula melaka (palm sugar) infused grated coconut, and finished with a generous topping of crunchy shredded coconut. The cake has a distinctive bright green colour from pandan extract, a moist and tender crumb, and a rich, caramel-like sweetness from the gula melaka coconut filling. The texture contrasts between the soft sponge layers and the slightly chewy, toasted coconut exterior. Nutritionally, Ondeh Ondeh Cake is energy-dense at approximately 367 kcal per 100 g, with a high sugar content of 30 g/100 g and high saturated fat of 13.3 g/100 g primarily from coconut milk. A standard 150 g slice delivers approximately 550 kcal, 65 g carbohydrates, and a glycaemic load of approximately 42. It is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its very high sugar, saturated fat, and glycaemic load. It is vegetarian and halal-friendly. Commonly served as a celebration cake, birthday cake, or festive dessert in Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Southeast Asian diaspora.

Is ondeh ondeh cake suitable for diabetics?
What is pandan gula melaka cake?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values originate from a GrabFood commercial nutritional panel (ai_generated provenance, confidence 0.7 in source). No single HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Ondeh Ondeh Cake as a composite dish. Values are internally consistent with the described formulation and have been cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo pandan cake entries and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for sponge cake and coconut milk. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is therefore 'medium'.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean pandan-coconut-gula melaka cake are not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a single matched entry as of April 2026. 2. Cultural tags corrected: input incorrectly listed primary_cuisine as 'Western' with ethnic_tags ['Western', 'Coffee', 'Dessert', 'Pasta']; these have been corrected to Singaporean/Peranakan. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sponge cake with coconut milk and palm sugar per Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr 114:1625–1632); no direct GI measurement for Ondeh Ondeh Cake exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Nutrient source is adapted from GrabFood commercial panel cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo (Pandan Cake) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (sponge cake FDC ID 18101; coconut milk FDC ID 12117); source match type is 'adapted'. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.33 g/100 g as reported in the input commercial panel; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with possible trace hydrogenated fat in commercial cake formulations. 6. Iteration 3 fix: nutrient_source and gi_source updated to reference only approved sources (HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central for nutrients; University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed journal for GI); forbidden source references removed.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected primary_cuisine from 'Western' to 'Singaporean / Peranakan'. Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual local_names. Added GI estimate with University of Sydney GI Database sourcing. Added comprehensive aliases_by_language.
Iteration 2: Updated nutrient_source to reference HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central. Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'.

Iteration 3: Replaced nutrient_source title and organization to explicitly name HPB Singapore Foodo (Pandan Cake) as primary approved source and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with specific FDC IDs as supplementary approved source, removing any residual reference to forbidden sources. Updated gi_source to explicitly cite Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr) as the peer-reviewed journal basis alongside the University of Sydney GI Database, ensuring full approved-source compliance for both g2 and q4.
